sections.heading: Object-Oriented Programming in Java
sections.content: OOP organizes code around objects that combine state (fields) and behavior (methods). In Java, classes define those objects and their contracts. The four pillars work together: • Encapsulation — protects internal state and business rules through a controlled API • Abstraction — exposes a useful contract while hiding incidental complexity • Inheritance — models stable type hierarchies with a genuine is-a relationship • Polymorphism — lets different implementations satisfy the same contract A useful design does not apply every pillar mechanically. The goal is to keep business rules close to the state they protect and to make change local and understandable.

sections.heading: From concepts to design decisions
sections.content: A pillar is useful only when it solves a concrete design problem. • Encapsulation: a Delivery exposes dispatch() and complete(), rather than a public setStatus(), so invalid transitions are rejected at the object boundary. • Polymorphism: notification channels share a contract, while each channel owns its provider-specific behavior. Adding a channel does not require editing existing handlers. • Inheritance: an exception hierarchy can express a stable is-a relationship; it is not a shortcut for injecting common dependencies into unrelated services. Evaluate each decision through the same lens: what changes independently, what rule must remain true, and what coupling does this introduce?

sections.heading: What it really means
Applied example
sections.content: A delivery begins as PLANNED, can be dispatched once, and can only be completed after dispatch. Those transitions belong to the entity because it owns the status. This design makes invalid states harder to create, gives one place to enforce the rule, and prevents callers from depending on the internal representation of status.

sections.heading: What it means
When NOT to abstract
sections.content: Not every class needs an interface. Create abstractions at meaningful boundaries: Abstract when: • a dependency is external infrastructure (database, messaging or third-party API) • multiple implementations exist or are a credible product need • the contract is owned by a different module or team • a stable test seam represents a real capability Avoid abstraction when: • it merely mirrors one concrete helper class • it adds a file without expressing a decision • the variation is speculative (YAGNI) Start with concrete code when the concept is unclear. Extract the smallest contract when its stable responsibility becomes visible.

sections.heading: When inheritance IS appropriate
When inheritance goes WRONG
sections.content:
Anti-pattern: BaseService for code reuse 
// ❌ Inheritance for CONVENIENCE, not is-a
abstract class BaseLogisticsService {
    @Inject protected EntityManager em;
    @Inject protected KafkaTemplate kafka;
    @Inject protected NotificationService notifier;
    @Inject protected AuditLogger audit;
    @Inject protected MetricsService metrics;
}

class DeliveryService extends BaseLogisticsService { ... }
class RouteService extends BaseLogisticsService { ... }
// 40+ services inherit 5 dependencies they may not all need
Problems: • All subclasses get ALL dependencies (even unused) • Changes to base class affect all 40+ subclasses (fragile base class) • Testing requires setting up all inherited dependencies • Not a real is-a relationship (DeliveryService is NOT a BaseLogisticsService) Fix: Composition 
// ✅ Each service declares ONLY what it needs
@Service
class DeliveryService {
    private final DeliveryRepository repo;
    private final LogisticsEventPublisher events;
    // Only 2 dependencies, not 5
}

sections.heading: Decision checklist
sections.content:
Before adding a superclass, ask:

• Can every subtype honor the parent contract without exceptions?
• Is the relationship semantic (is-a), rather than only shared implementation?
• Is the hierarchy likely to remain shallow and stable?
• Would injected collaborators make the dependencies more explicit?

If the answer to these questions is uncertain, composition is generally the safer default.

sections.heading: Runtime polymorphism
When polymorphism is NOT worth it
sections.content:
Don't use polymorphism when: • Only 2 simple cases with trivial logic → switch is clearer • Cases are stable and unlikely to grow • Each "implementation" is 1-2 lines • The indirection makes code harder to follow Use polymorphism when: • 3+ cases with independent, complex logic • New cases are added regularly • Each case has different dependencies • Cases need independent testing • You want Open/Closed (new behavior without modifying existing code) 
// ✅ Switch is FINE for this:
String label = switch (status) {
    case PLANNED -> "Waiting";
    case DISPATCHED -> "On the way";
    case DELIVERED -> "Done";
};

// ✅ Polymorphism is BETTER for this:
// 6+ notification channels, each with complex logic,
// different dependencies, growing every quarter

sections.heading: Design trade-off
sections.content: Polymorphism is valuable when behavior varies independently and each implementation has meaningful logic or dependencies. It keeps the dispatcher focused on orchestration while handlers own their behavior. For a closed, small and stable set of simple cases, an exhaustive switch is often clearer. Choose the smallest structure that makes future variation local.

sections.heading: What composition means
Why prefer composition over inheritance
sections.content:
InheritanceComposition
CouplingStrong — base change breaks allLoose — components independent
DependenciesInherits ALL (even unused)Declares ONLY what needed
TestingMust setup entire hierarchyMock only real dependencies
FlexibilitySingle hierarchy (Java)Compose from many sources
ReadabilityHidden inherited behaviorExplicit constructor dependencies
When to useStable is-a relationshipsHas-a / uses-a relationships
Connection to DI: Dependency Injection is the MECHANISM that enables composition. CDI (@Inject) or Spring (constructor injection) provides the components. The PRINCIPLE is composition; the TECHNIQUE is DI.

sections.heading: Design trade-off
sections.content:
Composition makes dependencies visible in the constructor and allows each collaborator to be replaced or tested independently. It is especially effective when services combine capabilities that change at different rates.

Its cost is a larger object graph and more wiring. Keep cohesive behavior together; introduce a collaborator when it owns a distinct responsibility, not merely to reduce the size of a class.

sections.heading: S — Single Responsibility Principle
O — Open/Closed Principle
L — Liskov Substitution Principle
I — Interface Segregation Principle
D — Dependency Inversion Principle
Senior Perspective
sections.content:
Senior engineers use OOP as a tool for managing complexity — not as a religion.

Key distinctions:
• Polymorphism enables extensibility when behavior varies behind a stable contract
• Inheritance creates coupling — prefer composition unless there is a genuine is-a relationship
• Encapsulation is about protecting invariants, not just making fields private
• The best OO designs have high cohesion (a class does one thing well) and low coupling (few dependencies)
• Design patterns are named solutions to OOP problems — useful vocabulary, not mandatory architecture

sections.heading: REWE Perspective
sections.content:
In TRAB's logistics domain:

• Delivery types (Express, Standard, Pickup) share behavior but vary in pricing and routing → polymorphism
• Route calculation strategies (shortest, fastest, cheapest) → Strategy pattern with polymorphic dispatch
• A Delivery is NOT a Route — it HAS a route → composition, not inheritance
• Vehicle types (truck, van, cargo bike) share a common interface but differ in capacity constraints

sections.heading: When NOT to use
sections.content:
When OOP is not the best approach:

• Pure data transformations (functional style is clearer)
• Simple scripts and glue code where classes add unnecessary ceremony
• Stateless utility functions that don't benefit from encapsulation
• Event processing pipelines where data flows through functions
• When the domain has no meaningful state or behavior to encapsulate
